data.title: H&E Equipment: Special Situation With A 10% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Since I sold a significant portion of my portfolio in late 2024, I have been looking for alternatives to allocate the funds.

The most obvious solutions have been U.S. Treasuries and high-yield corporate debt with short maturities, but some special situations also offer interesting returns.

In this article, I review the expected returns investors could get by holding H&E Equipment Services (NASDAQ:HEES) until the acquisition by Herc Holdings Inc. (NYSE:HRI) is completed.

Context

In August 2024, an informal conversation between executives of H&E and United Rentals (NYSE:URI) led to the idea of the acquisition.

At the time, H&E was trading at $48 per share (5x EBITDA).

After the initial suggestion by the United Rentals management that an “in the $80.00s” per share offer would be fair, the H&E management declined.

Negotiation went on until January 2025, when H&E accepted an offer for $92 per share.

However, the merger agreement included a 35-day go-shop period, which H&E used to contact 12 other companies seeking a higher bid. Two of them made an offer.

One of them was an offer from Herc Holdings, offering $78.75 per share plus 0.1287 shares of Herc per H&E share.

At the time, the offer represented a 14% premium to the United Rentals offer, which led management to accept it.

United Rentals decided not to submit a higher bid, receiving a $63.5M termination fee and showing disciplined capital allocation, as I explained in my recent coverage of the company.

The Deal

While H&E had a straightforward cash offer with United Rentals, the Herc deal is a bit more complicated, as it includes the stock consideration.

At the time of the offer, Herc was trading at $200.70 per share.

The stock consideration was valued at $25.80 per share, which added to the $78.75 offered in cash, resulted in a total consideration of $104.60 per share.

However, Herc would issue shares to cover the stock consideration, diluting the share count by 16.4%. The market rapidly adjusted the stock price, causing a significant decline in the following days.

After the acquisition, H&E shareholders will own 14.1% of Herc.

As I detailed in my latest Herc article where I assigned it a sell rating, my estimated fair value for the company after the acquisition is $115 per share.

At this price, which Herc is already approaching, the share consideration would be valued at $14.80. Added to the cash consideration, the final acquisition price would stand at $93.55 per H&E share.

Timing and Likelihood of Closing

One of the key aspects to determining the expected returns investors could obtain from this special situation is the timing and likelihood of the transaction closing.

For the transaction to close, half of the H&E shares have to be tendered into the offer. On that front, I do not see any risks.

Management has unanimously recommended accepting the offer, and directors combined own 12.5% of the company.

The premium is substantial, and the price offered is way above the industry average, as shown in the chart below.

For those reasons, I do not expect many shareholders to refuse the offer.

Timing-wise, the transaction is expected to close by mid-2025, as stated by H&E's management.

However, it will depend on regulatory approvals.

The initial expiration date was April 15, 2025, but it has already been extended until the regulatory review is underway.

The outside date is November 24, 2025, although it can be extended to February 2026.

The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) is currently reviewing the application to assess the impact on market competition.

Although the standard waiting period is 30 days, it is not unusual to take longer for larger transactions.

While the closing date is uncertain, the probability of closing from a regulatory perspective is high.

First, United Rentals has already studied the transaction before. Considering that United Rentals' market share (15%) is much larger than Herc's (4%), the transaction shouldn't raise any antitrust concerns.

Looking at similar transactions, there are no reasons for this one to get blocked by the FTC. The total consideration is ~$3.4B, and the combined market share after the acquisition would be at 6%.

As shown in the image below, larger transactions (especially when adjusted for inflation) have been approved in the past.

In addition, the merger agreement includes the obligation for Herc to divest assets if required.

Finally, the time between the submission to the FTC and the outside date is enough to get regulatory clearance.

Expected Returns

The main factors to determine the expected returns are the transaction closing date and the price of Herc's stock.

Considering the current stock price of H&E ($90.70 per share), if the deal is closed when Herc's stock is trading below $93 per share, the returns would be negative.

For this to happen, Herc's stock would need to decline by 20%.

In the rest of the scenarios, returns are positive, but the annual yield is also impacted by the timing.

In the transaction closing months column, I am considering the last day of each month.

The longer it takes for the transaction to close, the lower the annual yield.

If at the closing date, Herc is trading at current prices, which is the fair price I estimated in my coverage of the company, the annual yield would range from 16.5% if the transaction is closed at the end of June to 4.5% if it happens in December.

In my view, I assign the highest probabilities in July and August, with an average expected annual rate of 10%.

The current yield might be enough to justify the volatility risk, considering the likelihood of the deal closing and the current stock price of Herc.

Risks

The main risk for the thesis is a significant decline in equity markets.

If the current trade war situation escalates, and we see yet again an increase in volatility, the price of Herc's stock will decline, reducing the expected returns for H&E shareholders.

The other risk I identify is the delay in getting regulatory approval in combination with an economic slowdown.

If the transaction is not completed by November (could be extended to February 24, 2026) and a tough economic environment puts Herc in a difficult financial situation, the company might withdraw the offer.

Bottom Line

As Herc's stock approaches its fair value of $115 per share, H&E shareholders can expect an annual rate of return of 10% if the transaction is closed during July or August.

If the closing date is earlier, as management expects, returns are even greater.

However, the reader might not consider the current gap as big enough to justify the uncertainty around Herc's price.

In that case, a lower entry price would provide a higher margin of safety.

data.title: Farmers & Merchants Bancorp: A Dividend King, Unique Farming Risk
data.description:
This earnings season has been absolutely pivotal as we look for signs from the regional banks that the economy is showing signs of cracking. So far, some banks have shown some early signs of concerns, with deposits and loan demand down. However, other banks have been enjoying growth. The issue at hand? Yes, it is the so-called tariff risk.

Now here is the thing. While there is little direct impact, if regional customers, particularly on the business loan side of the bank, take a hit to their business, it could hurt loan demand or the ability to repay. And if revenue is down, it could hit deposits.

On the flip side, those with primarily consumer lending have next to no risk. But, if we see the economy worsen, we could see consumer spending slow, also slowing loan demand. So far, we have not seen it yet.

With that said, one of the regional banks we follow is in a special situation here. And that is the fiscal health of our farmers. In the short run, the tariff risk and trade war is seemingly harming our farmers. Longer-term the idea is for them to benefit American farmers, manufacturers, etc., but short term, there are big shocks, given that internationally sourced machinery and goods are adding costs. And with nations retaliating, it could mean less demand for our farmers' goods. Thus, we want to hone in on Farmers & Merchants Bancorp (OTCQX:FMCB).

This is a small, niche-type regional bank, with nearly three dozen branches across the Central Valley and East Bay areas in California. It is a major bank lender to agriculture in the U.S. We do like very much that this bank has raised its dividend for 60 consecutive years, and has paid dividends for 89 consecutive years. We have and do like it for dividend growth investing, but near term it faces some unique risks. The bank just reported Q1 earnings, and in this column, we examine the critical metrics we follow for regional banks.

In our opinion, the key metrics are holding up well, and the stock is only down about 5% during the selloff. In Q4, Farmers & Merchants Bancorp saw pockets of strength. Growth in deposits was 5.9% from the sequential Q4 hitting $5.0 billion. However, there was a slight decrease in total loans of 2.57% compared to Q4 2024. Many other regionals we have covered have seen declines in one or the other, so this is not so dramatic, but loans are down 3% from a year ago. Something to keep an eye on.

In terms of the risks to farming, CEO Kent Steinwert stated in the release:

Gross loans and leases were $3.6 billion at the end of the first quarter, down 2.56% from December 31, 2024 due in part to some seasonality in agricultural lending and due to our continued conservative approach in underwriting given the inverted yield curve which continues to not price in duration risk for loans and leases beyond three years. Credit quality remains solid and we continue to work closely with our borrowers as they work through the current economic cycle, particularly in a few agricultural products adversely impacted by negative conditions in the export market. However, the recent tariffs coupled with the weakened US dollar may improve export market opportunities for these products and may provide financial relief to affected customers.

This is insightful and explains that some loan demand decline was seasonal, but the bank has also tightened lending standards. As mentioned in the open, farmers face unique risks here. The export market has been tough, but the CEO sees changes to the export market and tariffs, as well as a weaker dollar helping farmers.

We also like that Farmers and Merchants expanded their margins. For Q1 2025 the net interest margin increased to 4.20% compared to 4.07% in the sequential Q4 2024 driven by a decrease in the average cost of total deposits. This drove net interest income to $53.1 million up $1 million from the sequential quarter. On the earnings front, this bank is incredibly profitable. Net income was $23.0 million, or $32.88 per share. This EPS was up 7.6% from a year ago and was a record Q1 for the company. For all of 2025, we are projecting $135 in EPS, putting the stock at just 7.5X FWD EPS. This is a very attractive price in our opinion.

The company has strong returns and asset quality. The return on average assets was 1.70%, up from 1.62 in the sequential Q4. Further, the return on average equity was 15.65% in the quarter. This was a 35 basis point improvement from Q4. The company also paid record dividends. Oh, and it is one of the most efficient banks. The efficiency ratio remains incredibly strong at 43.86%, also among the top in the industry. Very few regional banks have a sub-50% efficiency. While the credit quality remained incredibly strong, the allowance for credit losses was 2.10%, ticking up 6 basis points from Q4. There was a minor $300,000 provision for credit losses recorded in Q1, while non-accrual loans are just $193,000, while net charge-offs were $160,000 in the quarter.

Final thoughts

As of right now, the bank stock remains in a stable trading range. This is fine considering it is a dividend king given its history of dividend hikes. We continue to expect annual dividend hikes. We like the margins here, the income power, and the exceptional asset quality and efficiency.

While the bank does cater to agricultural customers which are under some short-term pressure, we are not seeing any major signs of trouble here yet. And, a weaker dollar could be a benefit to export demand.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Powell Has Backed Trump Into A Corner With The Market In The Crosshairs
data.description:
President Trump will wage his trade war without the Fed's help, and the stock market is likely to be the collateral damage. It appears that Fed Chair Jerome Powell's latest messaging is that the Fed is determined to prevent one-time price increases in the economy due to tariffs from becoming more persistent.

That said, Powell seems more than happy to let the stock market struggle and the economy slow down if Trump's tariffs are pushing up inflation rates. This will leave the weight of the trade war and the stock market's struggles all on President Trump and his trade policy.

Powell Would Be Justified In Not Cutting

There is little doubt that tariffs will cause a spike in inflation, especially given the size and scope of the tariffs currently proposed. It is likely to slow growth considerably over the coming year, and could even create a stagflation-type environment.

The market seems to think that the Fed will still cut the rate three times in 2025, based on December Fed Fund Futures trading at 3.5%. However, there is a good chance that if inflation starts to pick up again, the Fed will deliver no rate cuts in 2025, even if growth slows.

Much of the preliminary data suggests that inflation is accelerating and has continued in April. The Philly Fed and the Empire State prices paid both continued to rise, reaching levels not seen since the summer of 2022. While it has not appeared in the official CPI and PPI numbers yet, it would be surprising if it did not show up sometime later, as the CPI and PPI typically lag.

Inflation swaps are also pricing higher inflation rates, at least over the next one to two years. A 1-year inflation swap is trading at 3.15%, and a 2-year swap is trading at 2.7%. Both are up considerably since bottoming in early September 2024. If the predictive values of these market-based inflation expectations and price paid indexes are correct, the CPI and PPI are at a low point and are likely to rise significantly.

This will give Powell the ammunition needed not to cut rates even if the unemployment rate is rising, and economic growth is slowing. The latest summary of economic projections showed an estimated unemployment rate of 4.4% this year. Meanwhile, John Williams, the President of the NY Fed, noted that the unemployment rate could rise to between 4.5% and 5%, from its current 4.2%.

No Fed Put

If the Fed holds steady and doesn't cut rates, the focus will be squarely on Trump's policies, and it will be hard to deny that the tariffs and trade policies are causing the economic pain.

It also probably means the recent decline in the stock market will continue to, with the Fed not coming to the rescue. In a Q&A session on April 16, Chairman Jay Powell was explicitly asked about how the Fed put would be used, and the answer was no.

Powell's response should not have been a surprise, as the Fed's job is not to rescue the stock market every time it falls by a significant amount, such as 20 or 30%. It certainly was not the case in 2022, and it most likely won't be the case in 2025, either.

The key role of the Fed in the market is to ensure that markets are functioning adequately. When market functioning becomes disorderly, that is when the Fed is likely to step in and provide liquidity to the market if needed.

Currently, the market is functioning just fine and doesn't appear to be exhibiting much stress, at least since Trump put a 90-day pause on his proposed tariffs.

The message from Powell is clear: the Fed won’t be running to the rescue to buffer the blow from Trump’s trade war. Without monetary support, any market fallout will land squarely on the shoulders of the Trump administration.

There will be no Fed put this time.

data.title: Banner Corporation 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Banner Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Gold Versus Equities Or Gold Plus Equities - That Is The Question
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

The U.S. equity market delivered a surprisingly strong performance in 2024, with the S&P 500 Index finishing the year up 25%, defying recession fears, geopolitical uncertainty and the highest interest rates in over two decades. But as we stepped into Q1 2025, the tempo changed. The momentum that defined late 2024 didn't carry forward cleanly. Volatility returned, leadership rotated and investors were reminded that "past performance" doesn't chart the path ahead.

Notably, March 31, 2025, was the end of the quarter before the recent market swings catalyzed by the Trump Administration's evolving tariff announcements and policies. If anything, the market's up-and-down swings have increased further in magnitude after the April 2 "Liberation Day."

Meanwhile, gold, often dismissed as a relic in an era of digital innovation, quietly—and then loudly—reasserted its relevance. After notching all-time highs in 2024, it surged again in Q1 2025, reaching new records of nearly $3,170 per troy ounce.1 Far from just a crisis hedge, gold became a mirror to deep structural currents—real rates, currency skepticism and global demand shifts.

After Liberation Day, gold has largely held levels about $3,000 per troy ounce.2

The Most Recent 10 Years

First, we compare the price of gold and the S&P 500 Index on a return basis over the past 10 years. In figure 1:

  • Gold's price dropped in 2015, 2018 and 2021. These were not the strongest equity market years by and large, as the S&P 500 Index was down in 2018, slightly positive in 2015 and up 28.7% in 2021.
  • Gold's price appreciated more than 10% in 2017, 2019, 2020, 2023, 2024 and in the first quarter of 2025. Most of these years were strong on the equity side, but the first quarter of 2025 is signaling a divergence. Equities were down and gold was up more than 19%, but the markets have been trying to digest an ever-changing tariff backdrop from the Trump Administration during this time. So far in April 2025, this narrative has held true.

Figure 1: The Most Recent Years—Contrasting the Ups and Downs of Gold and U.S. Equities

The Longer-Term Trends

On a longer-term basis, we looked back to 1967 using quarterly rather than yearly data. One of the reasons for this is evident if you go back for a moment to figure 1 and look at 2020.

  • The yearly return for gold and equities in 2020 were both quite strong.
  • Using annual data, it is possible to forget how governments essentially shut down the global economy, putting many financial markets in free fall during the first quarter of 2020. While there was a subsequent recovery, we can see more extremes in the quarterly data as opposed to the smoother calendar year data.

In figure 2, we pulled out the 20 worst quarters of returns for the S&P 500 Index, going back to December 29, 1967.

  • Out of the 20 periods, five were associated with a negative return in the price of gold and 15 were associated with a positive return. The third quarter of 1975 was the worst, with gold actually down further than the S&P 500 Index. This was not, however, the usual case.

Figure 2: The 20 Worst Quarters of S&P 500 Index Performance from 1967

In figure 3, we flipped the script and looked at the 20 best quarters of returns for the S&P 500 Index over the same time frame.

  • Out of the 20 periods, seven were associated with a negative return on the price of gold. The second quarter of 1980 was the most unusual, in that it showed gold up more than 30% in a three-month period.

Figure 3: The 20 Best Quarters of S&P 500 Index Performance from 1967

As a bottom line, gold is not "always up" or "always down" or "always a good hedge" for various equity market scenarios. The historical track record is mixed.

Monthly Data

We also showcase characteristics of the monthly returns. In figure 4, we break out the historical record into four groupings based on the returns of the S&P 500 Index.

  • Worse than −5.0%
  • Between −5.0% and 0.0%
  • Between 0.0% and 5.0%
  • Above 5.0%

The two best groupings for gold were the extremes—observed either when the S&P 500 Index returned worse than −5.0% in a month or better than 5.0% in a month.

Figure 4: Monthly Data Indicated Gold Was More Interesting in Equity Market Extremes

The WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Equity Strategy Fund (GDE)

The aforementioned historical data shown in figures 1 to 4 considers gold and equities as separate asset classes—we are comparing the return of one to the return of another, given different scenarios. GDE allows us to think not of equities versus gold but rather of equities plus gold.

  • The exposure is defined as 90% invested in the 500 largest U.S. equities by market capitalization, with the other 10% in short-term U.S. Treasuries as collateral for a futures position.
  • The futures position is defined as a 90% notional exposure to gold futures.
  • The total exposure therefore includes the impact of leverage, as 90% exposure to equities + 90% exposure to gold futures = 180% combined exposure. Put another way, every $100 dollars is exposed to $180 notionally of equities and gold futures, split equally between the two.
  • Leverage has the potential to increase the volatility of an investment relative to either of the underlying asset classes viewed individually. GDE represents a long position in equities and a long position in gold futures, thereby benefitting if these asset classes are delivering positive returns.

Figure 5 depicts the standardized return picture.

Figure 5: Standardized Returns

GDE's track record has been strong when measured against the S&P 500 Index.

Figure 6: Strong Performance of GDE over its History

Conclusion: It Is Rare to Be #1

Morningstar places GDE within the broader U.S. Large Blend category. The leveraged exposure to gold futures is a unique proposition.

  • Year-to-date 2025, GDE was the second-best performing strategy within this peer group—and this was among 1,421 strategies in total.
  • Over the one-year period ended March 31, 2025, GDE was actually the TOP performing strategy in the very broad peer group—among 1,375 strategies.
  • For the three-year period ended March 31, 2025, GDE was also the TOP performing strategy, in this case among 1,274 different strategies.

Adding gold futures exposure on top of traditional equities is driving these results, but we think being the literal number-one fund in any time period in such a broad category is an impressive feat.

Figure 7: Ranking within the U.S. Large Blend Category

The challenge with adding gold historically: what part of the asset allocation pie you would sell to create room for it?

But GDE provides a solution to keep traditional equities while adding a useful portfolio diversifier. The last year is a good case study of how this worked versus other large blend solutions. Now when you face the question, Gold or equities? GDE can help you answer: Why not both!

1 Source: Gold - Price - Chart - Historical Data - News, as of 4/3/25.

2 Source: Brijesh Patel, "Gold Rises, Eyes Best Day since October 2023 as Tariff War Heats Up," Reuters, 4/9/25.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. The Fund is actively managed and invests in U.S.-listed gold futures and U.S. equity securities. The Fund’s use of U.S.-listed gold futures contracts will give rise to leverage, magnifying gains and losses and causing the Fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged. Moreover, the price movements in gold and gold futures contracts may fluctuate quickly and dramatically, and have a historically low correlation with the returns of the stock and bond markets. U.S. equity securities, such as common stocks, are subject to market, economic and business risks that may cause their prices to fluctuate. The Fund’s investment strategy will also require it to redeem shares for cash or to otherwise include cash as part of its redemption proceeds, which may cause the Fund to recognize capital gains. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree's London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU's Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor's degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post

data.title: Countries May Have To Choose Between China And The U.S.: Implications For Markets
data.description:
Introduction

As the trade war between China and the U.S. intensifies, there might be a world where countries have to choose with whom to trade. This was recently hinted at when a top President Trump official told Europe to choose between U.S. or Chinese communication tech. Additionally, Trump said on Fox News, “We may want countries to choose between the U.S. and China.”

All of this raises the question of what this will mean for markets. In this article, I am going to analyze which side countries will likely choose based on politics and trade connections. Further, I will show you the likely implications of different scenarios for the U.S. and China.

U.S. Trading Partners

Figure 1 shows the U.S. imports by country, while Figure 2 shows U.S. exports. As can be seen, the most important trading partners of the U.S. are: Mexico, Canada, China, Japan, the UK, Vietnam, South Korea, and the EU (while I would like to analyze all partners, this would be far too much for this article).

Starting with Mexico, the country would very likely choose the U.S. as a trading partner for obvious reasons, such as low transport costs and the importance of having a good relationship with its neighboring country. Additionally, the U.S. makes up 76% of exports and 42.8% of imports and is Mexico's biggest trading partner. The same argument works for Canada, which also has the U.S. as its by far most important trading partner.

China itself would, of course, stop trading with the U.S. in the scenario we are discussing. While Japanese exports are roughly equally distributed between China and the U.S., most of their imports come from China. On a political level, however, China and Japan have a long history of hating each other. This would make Japan more likely to side with the US, but due to the economic dependency, Japan will be put in the “undecided but likely U.S.” bucket for our future analysis.

While the UK sells most of its exports to the US, most of its imports come from China. Nevertheless, the gap in exports is much higher, while the UK and the US are allies and share roughly the same political views. Which would mean the UK would pick the US. In the EU, we can find the same trade structure as the UK, with exports going to the US and imports coming from China. Due to the recent tariffs, however, it is not impossible for the EU to choose China, which is why I put them in the “undecided but likely U.S.” bucket, like Japan.

South Korea would likely choose China, due to it being the largest trading partner and more near than the U.S. Nevertheless, it has a better relationship with the U.S., putting it in the “undecided but likely China” bucket. This brings us to Vietnam as the last important U.S. trading partner. They come in the bucket “undecided,” mainly because they are dependent on both partners.

Chinese Trading Partners

Figures 3 and 4 show the largest trading partners of China by imports and exports. Apart from the countries already discussed, important countries are: Australia, Russia, Brazil, Hong Kong, India, and Indonesia.

Russia and Hong Kong would pick China for obvious reasons. India comes in the “undecided” bucket, as it has important trade relations with both countries. Meanwhile, Indonesia would pick China as it is too dependent on the country in terms of trade. Brazil would very likely pick China as well for the same reason. Australia, surprisingly, would come into the “undecided but likely China” bucket. While it has strong ties to the U.S., the economic dependency could be too high for the country.

Analysis

Figure 5 shows a table listing all the named countries, their bucket, and their % of U.S. and Chinese exports and imports. This allows us now to build scenarios for the U.S. and China. The first scenario would be the standard scenario, meaning every country joins the country it is likely to join. For the completely undecided countries, the average will be taken. In this scenario, the U.S. would lose roughly 25% of its imports and 18% of its export partners. Using the same scenario for China, the country would lose roughly 35% of its import partners, while not exporting to 46% of its former export partners. While the base scenario would already be terrible for the U.S., China would be hit far harder. Interestingly, most of the pain would be based on whom the EU decided to trade with, as it makes up a lot of both countries' imports and exports.

In a best-case scenario, where every country decides for the U.S. that is not fully decided, the U.S. would lose 16.5% of its import partners and 11.5% of its export partners, while they would lose a tremendous amount of 55% of import and 43% of export partners in the worst-case scenario, where every undecided country would choose China.

In contrast, China would lose 10.6% of its import partners and 21.3% of its export partners in the best case. Additionally, they would lose 50.5% of imports and 56.8% of export partners in a worst-case scenario.

All of this shows the tremendous impact of countries having to choose with whom to trade.

Implications

So, what does this mean? Interestingly, it means that the EU is in a great position to make a deal at the moment. Losing the EU as a trading partner is something neither China nor the U.S. can allow themselves. This is also a reason why the scenario just analyzed is unlikely to happen, as long as a country is not 100% sure to have the support of the EU.

While most of the analysis looked worse for China than for the U.S., it would be terrible for both economies and currencies. While companies would earn far fewer profits and lose some of their supply chains, the currencies of both countries would be far less used and likely to crash. A solution for investors to hedge against such a scenario could be to short the Chinese currency, as it will likely be devalued anyway to counteract some of the effects of tariffs and increase economic activity.

data.title: Huntington Bancshares Incorporated 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Huntington Bancshares Incorporated in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Huntington Bancshares: Back To An Attractive Income Opportunity
data.description:
Today we continue our Q1 regional bank coverage with Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (NASDAQ:HBAN). We cover this stock every quarter, and our last take was suggesting you consider taking some profit of $17-$18 and looking to come back in sub-$14. With the market and sector selloff, we are back to these levels again, and we think you do some buying for income here, as the yield is an attractive 4.7%. Consider scaling in, buying in 50 cent blocks, from here down to $12 and then hold for income, Let us discuss the just-reported Q1 earnings.

Huntington Bancshares Q1 earnings headline performance

On the headline results, Huntington Bancshares exceeded expectations. The bank reported Q1 net income of $527 million, down $3 million from the sequential quarter, but better than expected. It was up a strong $108 million, or 26% from last year.

Folks this is a tremendous result. Adjusted earnings per common share were $0.34, flat from the sequential quarter, but were up $0.08 from a year ago.

Both loans and deposits see growth in Q1

We like a buy for income here because loans and total deposits continue to grow reliably. The bank is executing well. The best banks, the ones you want to own, grow loans and deposits and maintain strong asset quality. Huntington fits the bill.

Here in Q1, average total deposits increased $2.1 billion, or 1%, compared to the sequential Q4. These deposits were also up $10.9 billion, or 7%, from last year's Q1. On top of that, loan balances continue to grow. Average total loans and leases increased $2.7 billion (or 2%), from the sequential quarter to $130.9 billion. Compared to a year ago, these loans were up $8.9 billion, or 7%. So, while other regional banks we have covered so far have shown some weakness, we are not seeing it here with Huntington.

If the economy meaningfully worsens, we could see this change, but for now, activity remains strong. We also want to highlight that net interest margin increased another 7 basis points to 3.10% from 3.03% last quarter. This is very strong, and long term, the bank has enjoyed a higher net interest margin on average relative to peers.

Huntington Bancshares asset quality improves

Asset quality metrics improved in Q1. Net charge-offs were just 0.26% of average total loans and leases for the quarter, and this was a 4 basis point improvement from 0.30% in Q4. We continue to watch this very critical metric, and this decline is encouraging, as charge-offs are pretty stable while loans are going up.

That said, the allowance for credit losses has improved on a percentage basis for all loans as well. These are at $2.5 billion, or 1.87%, of total loans and leases at quarter end, a 1 basis point improvement. Finally, efficiency has always been strong here. Earlier in 2024, we opined that we expected the ratio to be back under 60%. We saw in Q3 2024 it was 59.4%, and 58.6% in Q4. It remained strong at 58.9% in Q1. This is a solid performance.

Huntington Bancshares valuation reset

We are seeing strong performance, but we have a new valuation here after the market selloff. From $18 to $13 offers an opportunity and is valued in our opinion for a quality buy here. We are back to a relatively high yield, trading at just 9.6X forward earnings and 1.5X tangible book value.

Final thoughts

This is a well-run bank, and shares are now a buy. We like scaling in, buying in 50 cent blocks here and down to $12. This will give you an average basis with over a 5% yield to hold for income.

data.title: Avanos Medical: Cheap, But With Caveats
data.description:
Today, we put Avanos Medical, Inc. (NYSE:AVNS) in the spotlight for the first time. This medical device concern named a new CEO in March that came over from Siemens Medical Solutions USA. The stock is also cheap on a P/E ratio, after falling nearly 25% year to date. Especially compared to the overall market where the S&P 500 (SP500) is trading just over 21 times forward earnings currently. Is the stock in the bargain bin? An analysis follows below.

Avanos Medical is headquartered in Alpharetta, GA. The company operates in many healthcare segments and sells primarily to hospitals, third-party distributors, healthcare facilities and other healthcare providers. These include offers digestive health products including neonatal feeding offerings, non-opioid pain solutions, including surgical pain and recovery products as well as radiofrequency ablation products to treat chronic pain conditions and knee osteoarthritis hyaluronic acid pain relief injection products. The stock currently trades at around $12.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $550 million.

The company has two main divisions Enteral Feeding and Pain Management & Recovery. The new CEO joints Avanos Medical as the company was already going through some transition. The company sold its struggling Respiratory Health business to SunMed Group in the summer of 2023. Around the same time, Avanos purchased Toronto-based Diros Technology Inc. with its radiofrequency products portfolio.

Of note, in the fourth quarter, Avanos finished all their obligations to perform certain manufacturing services in connection with the sale of its Respiratory Health business. All assets that were previously accounted for as 'held for sale' were officially transferred to SunMed on October 1st, 2024. In addition, in January the company received notification from the DOJ that Avanos met their commitments with regard to their deferred prosecution agreement. Avanos will pay a $22 million fine for misbranding its Microcool surgical gowns.

Recent Results:

Avanos Medical posted its Q4 numbers on February 26th. The company delivered non-GAAP of 43 cents a share, three pennies a share above expectations. Gross margin fell slightly to 54.6% from 54.8% in the same period a year ago.

Adjusted EBITDA fell to $28.6 million from $32.1 million. Adjusted EBITDA for FY2024 for continuing operations was $108 million. On a GAAP basis, Avanos took a non-cash impairment charge of $437 million. This was based upon an assessment that the fair value of Avanos' single company reporting unit was below the unit's carrying value. For FY2024, Avanos booked non-GAAP earnings of $1.35 a share, up 31% from FY2023.

Revenues rose 3.6% on a year-over-year to $179.6 million, beating expectations by some $2 million. Enteral Feeding drove the revenue increase as sales grew to $106 million from $94 million in the same period a year ago. Pain Management sales fell to $74 million from $77 million.

Management provided the following guidance for FY2025.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Avanos Medical gets little coverage from Wall Street currently. Since the company's fourth quarter results hit the wires, both JMP Securities and Stifel Nicolaus ($17 price target, down from $20 previously) maintained Hold ratings on the stock. That is the only analyst firm commentary I can find on Avanos Medical in 2025.

Just under two percent of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. One company officer sold just over $105,000 worth of stock in March. That is the only insider activity in the stock year to date. Several insiders sold approximately $200,000 worth of equity in 2024, collectively. There was no insider activity in the shares 2021 through 2023.

Avanos ended the fiscal 2024 with a net debt of $27 million after starting the year with a net debt of just over $80 million. The company produced $82.9 million in free cash flow in FY2024, of which $53.1 million came in Q4. It should be noted that $18 million of free cash came from a tax receivable. Avanos announced a $25 million stock buyback authorization program in late November. The restructuring efforts of the past two years have been a key driver of improved cash flow.

Conclusion:

Avanos Medical made $1.35 a share in FY2024 on just under $678 million of revenues. The current analyst firm consensus has profits falling to $1.12 a share as sales decrease slightly to $673 million in FY2025. They project earnings rebounding to $1.27 a share on three to four percent revenue growth in FY2026.

The company has done a commendable job in bringing down leverage and substantially improving free cash flow. The stock is not expensive at nine times trailing earnings. The shares do not pay a dividend and are thinly traded, it should be noted. There is little liquidity in the options market against the equity, taking a covered call strategy off the table.

In addition, both sales and earnings are projected to decline in FY2025. While the company does not break down international sales, it does sell globally. Avanos also sources products from Mexico, Canada, and China. Therefore, there are significant tariff policy unknowns for the company which management referenced in its fourth quarter conference call.

Given all of this, I have Avanos on my "watch list," but don't plan to take a stake in it until the company returns to growth and tariff impacts are more known.

data.title: Down 20%, Still A Buy: Why I'm Doubling Down On UnitedHealth Group
data.description:
Thesis Summary

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) is down 20% after a Q1 earnings miss and lowered guidance. But is this selloff justified?

The company is undoubtedly dealing with some issues, but this is, in my opinion, a market overreaction.

The stock is now back where it was a month ago, and I believe this is a good time to buy.

I remain bullish on the back of the company’s compelling defensive revenue streams, earnings forecasts, and favorable valuation.

UNH continues to be a very solid company, a good defensive buy to own in hard times, even if today it doesn’t seem like it.

I rate UNH a Strong Buy after the selloff.

Q1 Overview

UNH posted decent earnings, with revenue up 10% YoY and adjusted EPS of $7.20. Operating earnings were $9.1/share.

However, the company’s full-year guidance was lowered. The company now expects full-year EPS of around $26.

This is due to higher expected costs associated with Medicare Advantage care activity and OptumHealth reimbursement headwinds.

Still, we saw the company add 780,000 members YTD and Optum Health expects to serve 650,000 more “value-based care patients” by year-end.

It’s worth noting that Optum Revenue was down sequentially, while UNH revenue saw a strong increase of 18%.

While UNH is the “traditional” insurance business, Optum is a health service that looks to serve patients through data analytics and technology.

Why I Still Like UNH

I think this is a great and rare opportunity to buy a quality stock at a favorable price.

Defensive Leader

UNH is a market leader in the healthcare sector, which is a defensive sector that does well even in recessions. The risk of a recession or even stagflation has dramatically risen in the last few months, which is why defensive stocks, like UNH, have actually outperformed recently.

While the earnings change the short-term outlook, the long-term appeal doesn’t change. Healthcare is resilient to downturns and even inflation as it provides a necessary service.

Growth And Stability

In this regard, UNH offers the best of both worlds, with steady revenue from UNH and some high-growth potential from Optum.

The segment launched in 2011 breaks down into three divisions, Optum Health, Optum Insight and Optum RX.

In my opinion, it allows UNH to capture an underserved market, while leveraging new technologies like AI.

There’s clearly a demand, with Optum Insight boasting a $32.9B backlog.

On top of that, these revenues have higher margins than the insurance business.

Historically Undervalued

Based on historical levels, UNH is now undervalued. With a P/E of around 17, this is below the 10-year average of 20 and the PEG ratio is now under 1.5, also compelling.

Ultimately, if you believe that current estimates are roughly accurate, projecting double-digit growth, then this is a great investment.

Add to these buybacks, dividends and a strong balance sheet, and you have a great long-term investment.

Technical Analysis

From a technical perspective, the price action in the coming days following the earnings will be very interesting.

UNH is set to open below the 200-week EMA around $465. This level has been broken a couple of times in the last few years, but the dip hasn’t lasted long.

Will this time be the exception?

We have some support around the previous low at $460, but below that could open the doors to some pain.

It will certainly be very interesting to see how different indicators like the RSI and MACD look in the coming week.

Risks

Of course, the recent earnings have given us reason for concern. Margins have been squeezed due to rising medical costs, especially in Medicare Advantage. Will this trend persist?

On the other hand, there’s definitely some uncertainty and risk related to policy, such as changes in Medicaid. This administration is certainly not afraid of shaking things up.

Final Thoughts

In conclusion, the current selloff in UNH seems overblown, since it follows only a small earnings miss. There could be a combination of factors at play here, such as the fact that the stock was already overstretched, and perhaps increased overall fear and volatility in the market.

But that doesn’t change the fact that UNH is a strong defensive play, which will, in fact, help hedge your portfolio from an economic downturn.
